questions.question: What problem does HTTP/2 multiplexing solve that HTTP/1.1 cannot?
options: HTTP/1.1 doesn't support encryption
HTTP/1.1 causes head-of-line blocking — one slow response blocks all others on the connection
HTTP/1.1 doesn't support binary data
HTTP/1.1 cannot reuse connections between requests
questions.explanation: HTTP/1.1 pipelining sends multiple requests but responses must arrive in order. If the first response is slow (large image, slow database query), every response behind it waits — head-of-line blocking. HTTP/2 multiplexes independent streams so a slow stream doesn't block others.

questions.question: How many TCP connections does a browser typically open to load a modern web page with HTTP/1.1?
options: 1 — keep-alive reuses it
2 — one for HTML, one for assets
6–8 — browsers work around head-of-line blocking by opening multiple connections
One per resource
questions.explanation: Browsers open 6 connections per origin (RFC 7230 suggests up to 2, browsers ignore this). Each connection avoids head-of-line blocking for its own requests. HTTP/2 eliminates this need — one connection handles all resources in parallel streams.

questions.question: What is an HTTP/2 stream?
options: A persistent TCP connection to the server
A binary WebSocket channel
An independent, bidirectional sequence of frames within a single TCP connection
A server-sent event channel
questions.explanation: In HTTP/2, each request/response pair gets a stream ID. Frames for different streams are interleaved on the same TCP connection. The receiver reassembles each stream by ID. This is multiplexing: many logical channels over one physical connection.

questions.question: What does HPACK do in HTTP/2?
options: Compresses the request body using gzip
Encrypts headers before TLS
Compresses HTTP headers using a shared dynamic table to avoid sending repeated headers
Packs multiple requests into a single frame to reduce round trips
questions.explanation: HPACK is HTTP/2's header compression format. Both client and server maintain a dynamic table of header name/value pairs seen in previous requests. Repeated headers (like Cookie, Accept-Encoding) are replaced by a small index reference instead of being sent in full every time.

questions.question: Why does HTTP/2 still suffer from head-of-line blocking at the TCP level?
options: HTTP/2 does not use TCP
TCP delivers bytes in order — a lost packet stalls all HTTP/2 streams until it's retransmitted
HTTP/2 streams share a flow control window that gets exhausted
TLS adds head-of-line blocking for all encrypted protocols
questions.explanation: HTTP/2 solved application-layer head-of-line blocking, but TCP still delivers bytes in order. If one packet is lost, TCP holds back all subsequent data (including data for unrelated HTTP/2 streams) until the retransmit arrives. HTTP/3 (QUIC) solves this by using UDP and implementing per-stream reliability.
